action: Install Node.js
detailed_instructions: Go to https://nodejs.org/ in your browser. Click the big green button that says 'LTS' (Long Term Support). This downloads an installer. Run the installer. IMPORTANT: During installation, check any boxes that say 'Add to PATH' or 'Automatically install tools'. Click Next through the rest of the wizard and finish the install.
inputs: None - just download and run the installer
outputs: Node.js and npm are now installed on your machine. 'npm' is a package manager that comes bundled with Node.js - it lets you install code libraries.
how_to_verify: Open a terminal (on Windows: search for 'Command Prompt' or 'PowerShell' in the Start menu and open it). Type: node --version and press Enter. You should see something like 'v18.20.0' or 'v20.11.0'. Then type: npm --version and press Enter. You should see a number like '9.8.1'.
troubleshooting: If 'node' is not recognized: close and reopen your terminal. If still not working, uninstall Node.js, reinstall it, and make sure you check 'Add to PATH' during installation.

action: Install Python
detailed_instructions: Go to https://python.org/ in your browser. Click 'Downloads' then click the big yellow button for the latest Python 3.12+ version. Run the installer. CRITICAL: On the very first screen of the installer, check the box at the bottom that says 'Add Python to PATH'. Then click 'Install Now'.
inputs: None - just download and run the installer
outputs: Python and pip are installed. 'pip' is Python's package manager - it lets you install Python libraries.
how_to_verify: In your terminal type: python --version (on Mac/Linux try: python3 --version). You should see 'Python 3.12.x' or higher. Then type: pip --version (or pip3 --version). You should see a version number.
troubleshooting: If 'python' is not recognized: you forgot to check 'Add Python to PATH'. Uninstall Python, reinstall, and CHECK THAT BOX.

action: Install AWS CLI
detailed_instructions: Go to https://aws.amazon.com/cli/ in your browser. Click 'Download and install the AWS CLI'. Choose the installer for your operating system (Windows/Mac/Linux). Run the installer with default settings.
inputs: None - just download and run the installer
outputs: The AWS CLI is installed. This is a command-line tool that lets you interact with all AWS services from your terminal.
how_to_verify: In your terminal type: aws --version and press Enter. You should see something like 'aws-cli/2.15.0'.
troubleshooting: If 'aws' is not recognized: close and reopen your terminal. On Windows you may need to restart your computer.

action: Create an AWS Account (if you don't have one)
detailed_instructions: Go to https://aws.amazon.com/ and click 'Create an AWS Account'. Follow the signup process. You will need: an email address, a credit card (you'll only be charged for what you use), and a phone number for verification. After signup, you are logged in as the 'root user'.
inputs: Your email address and a credit card
outputs: An AWS account. Your 12-digit Account ID is shown in the top-right dropdown of the console.
how_to_verify: Sign in at https://console.aws.amazon.com/ and you see the AWS Management Console dashboard.
troubleshooting: AWS accounts are free to create. You only pay for services you use. This project costs roughly $20-50/month.

action: Create an IAM User with Admin Access
detailed_instructions: In the AWS Console (https://console.aws.amazon.com/), search for 'IAM' in the top search bar and click it. Click 'Users' in the left sidebar. Click 'Create user'. Enter a username (e.g. 'rag-tool-admin'). Click Next. Select 'Attach policies directly'. Search for 'AdministratorAccess' and check its box. Also search for 'AWSMarketplaceFullAccess' and check its box. Click Next, then Create user.
inputs: Username you choose (e.g. 'rag-tool-admin')
outputs: A new IAM user with full admin permissions. IAM (Identity and Access Management) controls who can do what in your AWS account.
how_to_verify: You see the new user listed on the IAM Users page.
troubleshooting: Never share your root account credentials. Always use IAM users for daily work.

action: Create Access Keys for your IAM User
detailed_instructions: On the IAM Users page, click your new username. Click the 'Security credentials' tab. Scroll down to 'Access keys' and click 'Create access key'. Select 'Command Line Interface (CLI)' as the use case. Check the confirmation box and click Next, then Create. IMPORTANT: Copy both the 'Access key ID' and 'Secret access key' NOW and save them somewhere safe (e.g. a password manager). You will NOT be able to see the secret key again after closing this page.
inputs: Your IAM username from step 5
outputs: Two strings: an Access Key ID (looks like AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE) and a Secret Access Key (a longer random string). These are like a username and password for the AWS CLI.
how_to_verify: You can see the Access Key ID listed under the Security credentials tab of your IAM user. The Secret Key is only shown once at creation time.
troubleshooting: If you lose the Secret Key, delete the access key and create a new one. Never put these keys in code or share them publicly.

action: Configure the AWS CLI with your credentials
detailed_instructions: Open your terminal. Run: aws configure and press Enter. It will ask you 4 questions. Type each answer and press Enter after each one: (1) AWS Access Key ID: paste the Access Key ID from step 6. (2) AWS Secret Access Key: paste the Secret Access Key from step 6. (3) Default region name: type us-east-1 (4) Default output format: type json
inputs: Access Key ID and Secret Access Key from step 6. Region: us-east-1. Output: json.
outputs: Your terminal is now connected to your AWS account. Every 'aws' command you run will use these credentials.
how_to_verify: Run: aws sts get-caller-identity and press Enter. You should see JSON output containing your Account number and the username you created. Example output: {"UserId": "AIDA...", "Account": "123456789012", "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/rag-tool-admin"}
troubleshooting: If you get 'InvalidClientTokenId': you entered the wrong Access Key. Run 'aws configure' again and re-enter the keys carefully.

action: Install the AWS CDK CLI
detailed_instructions: In your terminal run: npm install -g aws-cdk and press Enter. This uses npm (installed with Node.js in step 1) to install the CDK globally on your machine. CDK (Cloud Development Kit) is a tool that creates AWS infrastructure from code - it converts TypeScript code into real AWS resources.
inputs: None
outputs: The CDK CLI is installed globally. You can now use the 'cdk' command to deploy AWS infrastructure.
how_to_verify: Run: cdk --version and press Enter. You should see a version like '2.170.0'.
troubleshooting: If 'npm' is not recognized, Node.js wasn't installed properly. Go back to step 1.

action: Open the AWS Console in the correct region
detailed_instructions: Open https://console.aws.amazon.com/ in your browser and sign in. Look at the top-right corner of the page next to your username - you'll see a region name. Click it and select 'US East (N. Virginia) us-east-1'. This is critical because the AI models we need are in this region.
inputs: Your AWS login credentials
outputs: You are signed in to the AWS Console in the us-east-1 region.
how_to_verify: The region selector in the top-right shows 'N. Virginia' or 'us-east-1'.
troubleshooting: If you deploy in the wrong region, the AI models won't be available and nothing will work.

action: Navigate to the Bedrock Model catalog
detailed_instructions: In the search bar at the very top of the AWS Console, type 'Bedrock' and press Enter. Click on 'Amazon Bedrock' from the results. On the left sidebar, click 'Model catalog'. This page shows all the AI models available to you.
inputs: None
outputs: You see the Model catalog page showing AI models from providers like Amazon, Anthropic, Meta, etc.
how_to_verify: The page title says 'Model catalog' and you see model cards/tiles.
troubleshooting: If you don't see 'Model catalog' in the sidebar, make sure you clicked on 'Amazon Bedrock' (not a different service).

action: Enable access to Claude models (one-time form)
detailed_instructions: In the Model catalog, search for or scroll to find any Anthropic Claude model (try 'Claude 3.5 Haiku'). Click on it. If this is your first time using Anthropic models, you will see a 'First Time Use' form. Fill it out: Company name (your company or 'Personal'), Website (your site or 'N/A'), Intended users (select 'Internal'), Industry (pick the closest match), Use case description (type: 'Internal document search and generation tool'). Click Submit.
inputs: Company name, website, industry, use case description
outputs: Anthropic Claude models are now enabled in your account. This form is required once per AWS account for all Claude models.
how_to_verify: After submitting, you should see a confirmation message. To double-check: go to Bedrock > Playground (left sidebar), select Claude 3.5 Haiku as the model, type 'Hello' and click Run. If Claude responds, access is working.
troubleshooting: If you do NOT see a form, your account already has access (someone else may have done this). If Claude gives an error in the Playground, your IAM user may be missing the 'AWSMarketplaceFullAccess' policy from step 5.

action: Find your public IP address
detailed_instructions: Open a new browser tab and go to https://whatismyip.com. Look for your 'IPv4 Address' on the page. It will be a set of 4 numbers separated by dots (e.g. 203.0.113.45). Write this down or copy it.
inputs: None
outputs: Your public IPv4 address (e.g. 203.0.113.45). This is the IP address the internet sees when you browse the web.
how_to_verify: The website displays your IP clearly on the page.
troubleshooting: If the site shows an IPv6 address (contains colons like 2001:db8::1), look specifically for the IPv4 address. Some sites show both.

action: Edit the IP allowlist in the project code
detailed_instructions: Navigate to the project folder. Open the file cdk/lib/config.ts in any text editor (Notepad, VS Code, etc). Find the line that says '0.0.0.0/32' (around line 55-65 inside the ALLOWED_IPS array). Replace '0.0.0.0/32' with your IP address from step 12 followed by /32. For example, if your IP is 203.0.113.45, the line should read: '203.0.113.45/32'. Save the file.
inputs: Input: your IP address from step 12 (e.g. 203.0.113.45). File to edit: cdk/lib/config.ts
outputs: The config file now contains your real IP. Only this IP (and any others you add) will be able to access the application. Everyone else gets blocked.
how_to_verify: Open the file and verify the ALLOWED_IPS line shows your IP with /32 at the end. The /32 means 'this exact single IP address'.
troubleshooting: If you need multiple people to access the app, add each IP on a new line inside the array. Example: '203.0.113.45/32', '198.51.100.22/32'. If your IP changes (common with home internet), you'll need to update this and redeploy.

action: Build the Python Lambda Layer
detailed_instructions: Open your terminal. Navigate to the backend folder inside the project: cd path/to/AWSClaude/backend (replace path/to with the actual path where you put the project files). Then run these two commands one at a time: (1) mkdir -p layers/dependencies/python (2) pip install -r requirements.txt -t layers/dependencies/python/
inputs: Input: None (the requirements.txt file lists what to install). Working directory: the backend/ folder of the project.
outputs: A new folder is created at backend/layers/dependencies/python/ containing Python libraries (pypdf for reading PDFs, python-docx for reading Word files). These get packaged into your Lambda functions so they can process documents.
how_to_verify: Run: ls layers/dependencies/python/ (or 'dir layers\dependencies\python' on Windows). You should see folders like 'pypdf', 'docx', etc.
troubleshooting: If pip gives errors: make sure Python is installed (step 2). If you see 'command not found' for pip, try 'pip3' instead. Ignore yellow warning messages about pip version - those are harmless.

action: Install CDK project dependencies
detailed_instructions: In your terminal, navigate to the CDK folder: cd ../cdk (or cd path/to/AWSClaude/cdk if starting from scratch). Then run: npm install
inputs: Input: None (package.json lists what to install). Working directory: the cdk/ folder.
outputs: A node_modules/ folder is created inside cdk/ containing all the CDK libraries needed to deploy infrastructure.
how_to_verify: You see 'added X packages' in the terminal output with no errors. A node_modules folder exists in the cdk/ directory.
troubleshooting: If npm gives errors: make sure Node.js is installed (step 1). If you see permission errors, try running your terminal as Administrator (Windows) or prefix with sudo (Mac/Linux).

action: Bootstrap CDK in your AWS account
detailed_instructions: From the cdk/ directory, run: cdk bootstrap. This is a one-time setup step that creates a special S3 bucket and IAM roles in your AWS account that CDK needs to function. Think of it as preparing your AWS account to receive CDK deployments.
inputs: Input: None. CDK reads your AWS credentials from step 7 automatically.
outputs: A CloudFormation stack called 'CDKToolkit' is created in your AWS account containing a staging S3 bucket and deployment roles.
how_to_verify: Terminal shows 'Environment aws://XXXXXXXXXXXX/us-east-1 bootstrapped (no changes)' or similar success message.
troubleshooting: If you see 'Unable to resolve AWS account': your AWS CLI is not configured. Go back to step 7. If you see 'Access Denied': your IAM user needs AdministratorAccess.

action: Deploy the core infrastructure (first pass - without Knowledge Base IDs)
detailed_instructions: From the cdk/ directory, run: cdk deploy RagToolStack --require-approval broadening. CDK will show you what it's about to create and ask for confirmation. Type 'y' and press Enter. Then wait 5-10 minutes while it creates: 2 S3 storage buckets, 6 Lambda serverless functions, an API Gateway for routing web requests, a CloudFront CDN for serving the website, and a WAF firewall for IP protection.
inputs: Input: None. CDK reads the config.ts file you edited in step 13.
outputs: A complete AWS infrastructure stack is created. After deployment, the terminal prints output values you'll need later. IMPORTANT: At this point the search/generation features won't work yet because we haven't connected the Knowledge Base. That happens in step 30.
how_to_verify: The terminal shows 'RagToolStack' with a green checkmark and prints outputs including: DistributionUrl (your website URL), DocumentsBucketName (where documents go), FrontendBucketName (where the website files go), DistributionId (for updating the website), ApiUrl (the API endpoint). Write all of these down!
troubleshooting: If CDK asks 'Do you wish to deploy these changes?' - type y. If deployment fails with IAM errors: make sure your user has AdministratorAccess. If it fails with 'stack already exists': someone already ran this - check the AWS CloudFormation console.

action: Save all the output values from deployment
detailed_instructions: After step 17 completes, the terminal prints several output values. Copy ALL of them and save them in a text file or note. They look like: RagToolStack.DistributionUrl = https://d1234567890abc.cloudfront.net | RagToolStack.DocumentsBucketName = 123456789012-rag-documents | RagToolStack.FrontendBucketName = 123456789012-rag-frontend | RagToolStack.DistributionId = E1234567890ABC | RagToolStack.ApiUrl = https://abc123.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com. If you missed them, run: aws cloudformation describe-stacks --stack-name RagToolStack --query Stacks[0].Outputs --output table
inputs: Input: None - just read the terminal output from step 17.
outputs: You have 5 values saved: DistributionUrl, DocumentsBucketName, FrontendBucketName, DistributionId, ApiUrl. You will need these in later steps.
how_to_verify: You have all 5 values written down somewhere accessible.
troubleshooting: You can always retrieve these values later by running: aws cloudformation describe-stacks --stack-name RagToolStack --query Stacks[0].Outputs --output table

action: Create category folders in your documents bucket
detailed_instructions: Run these 4 commands in your terminal. Replace YOUR_BUCKET with the DocumentsBucketName you saved in step 18 (e.g. 123456789012-rag-documents). Run each command one at a time: (1) aws s3api put-object --bucket YOUR_BUCKET --key tech-approach/ --content-length 0 (2) aws s3api put-object --bucket YOUR_BUCKET --key organizational-approach/ --content-length 0 (3) aws s3api put-object --bucket YOUR_BUCKET --key past-performance/ --content-length 0 (4) aws s3api put-object --bucket YOUR_BUCKET --key resumes/ --content-length 0
inputs: Input: DocumentsBucketName from step 18 (e.g. 123456789012-rag-documents)
outputs: Four category folders are created inside your S3 bucket. S3 doesn't have real folders - these commands create empty marker files that act as folder dividers.
how_to_verify: Each command prints a small JSON response with an 'ETag' value. That means success. You can also verify in the AWS Console: go to S3, click your documents bucket, and you should see the 4 folders.
troubleshooting: If you get 'NoSuchBucket': double-check the bucket name. It should match DocumentsBucketName exactly from step 18.

action: Upload your reference documents to S3
detailed_instructions: For each document you want the tool to search through, upload it to the appropriate category folder. Run: aws s3 cp FILENAME s3://YOUR_BUCKET/CATEGORY/ -- Replace FILENAME with the path to your file, YOUR_BUCKET with DocumentsBucketName from step 18, and CATEGORY with one of: tech-approach, organizational-approach, past-performance, or resumes. Examples: aws s3 cp proposal.pdf s3://123456789012-rag-documents/tech-approach/ | aws s3 cp john-resume.pdf s3://123456789012-rag-documents/resumes/ | To upload an entire folder at once: aws s3 sync ./my-tech-docs/ s3://YOUR_BUCKET/tech-approach/
inputs: Input: Your document files (.pdf, .docx, .txt) and the DocumentsBucketName from step 18.
outputs: Your documents are stored in S3 organized by category. These are the documents the RAG tool will search through when users ask questions.
how_to_verify: Run: aws s3 ls s3://YOUR_BUCKET/tech-approach/ to see files in a category. Each file should be listed with its size and date.
troubleshooting: If you get 'upload failed': check the filename path is correct. Use quotes around filenames with spaces. Make sure the bucket name is exact.

action: Open the Knowledge Bases page in the AWS Console
detailed_instructions: Go to the AWS Console (https://console.aws.amazon.com/). Make sure you're in us-east-1 (check top-right). Search for 'Bedrock' and click Amazon Bedrock. In the left sidebar, look under 'Orchestration' and click 'Knowledge bases'.
inputs: None
outputs: You see the Knowledge bases page. This might be empty if you haven't created one yet.
how_to_verify: The page title says 'Knowledge bases'.
troubleshooting: If you don't see 'Knowledge bases' in the sidebar, try expanding the 'Orchestration' section.

action: Start creating a new Knowledge Base
detailed_instructions: Click the 'Create' button. Then select 'Create a knowledge base with a vector store'. This starts a multi-step wizard that will set up the entire RAG pipeline for you.
inputs: None
outputs: The Knowledge Base creation wizard opens at Step 1 (Knowledge base details).
how_to_verify: You see a form asking for a name and description.
troubleshooting: If you don't see the 'Create' button, check that you are in us-east-1 region.

action: Fill in Knowledge Base details (wizard step 1 of 4)
detailed_instructions: In the Name field type: rag-tool-kb. In the Description field type: RAG document search knowledge base. Under IAM permissions select 'Create and use a new service role' (this lets Bedrock automatically create the permissions it needs). Leave everything else as default. Click the 'Next' button at the bottom.
inputs: Input: Name: rag-tool-kb | Description: RAG document search knowledge base | IAM: Create and use a new service role
outputs: The wizard saves your KB name and moves to Step 2 (Data source).
how_to_verify: You see the Data source configuration page (Step 2).
troubleshooting: If Next is grayed out, make sure you filled in the Name field.

action: Configure the S3 data source (wizard step 2 of 4)
detailed_instructions: In Data source name type: rag-documents-s3. Under Data source type select 'Amazon S3'. Click the 'Browse S3' button and find your documents bucket (the DocumentsBucketName from step 18 - it looks like 123456789012-rag-documents). Click on it to select it. Leave all other settings as defaults. Click 'Next'.
inputs: Input: Data source name: rag-documents-s3 | S3 bucket: your DocumentsBucketName from step 18
outputs: The wizard knows where your documents are stored (in your S3 bucket) and moves to Step 3.
how_to_verify: You see the Embeddings model configuration page (Step 3).
troubleshooting: If you can't find your bucket: make sure you're in us-east-1. The bucket was created in step 17.

action: Select the embeddings model (wizard step 3 of 4)
detailed_instructions: Select 'Titan Text Embeddings V2' from the list of embedding models. This is Amazon's own model that converts text into numerical vectors (arrays of numbers that capture the meaning of the text). Leave the vector dimensions as 1024 (this is the length of each vector - higher means more accurate but uses more storage). Click 'Next'.
inputs: Input: Model: Titan Text Embeddings V2 | Dimensions: 1024
outputs: The wizard knows which AI model to use for converting your documents into searchable vectors. Moves to Step 4.
how_to_verify: You see the Vector database configuration page (Step 4).
troubleshooting: Titan Embed V2 is free to select. You pay a tiny amount per use (~$0.00011 per 1000 words).

action: Choose the vector database (wizard step 4 of 4)
detailed_instructions: Select 'Quick create a new vector store'. Then choose 'Amazon S3 Vectors'. This is the cheapest storage option for your vectors. It costs about $0.06 per GB per month and scales to zero when not in use (you don't pay if nobody is searching). Leave all defaults. Click 'Next'.
inputs: Input: Type: Quick create | Store: Amazon S3 Vectors
outputs: The wizard knows where to store the vector embeddings. Moves to the Review page.
how_to_verify: You see a Review page showing all your choices.
troubleshooting: S3 Vectors is recommended for small teams. OpenSearch Serverless is more powerful but costs a minimum of ~$700/month even when idle.

action: Review and create the Knowledge Base
detailed_instructions: Look over all the settings on the Review page to make sure they match what you entered. Then click 'Create knowledge base' at the bottom. Wait 1-3 minutes for the status to change to 'Ready' or 'Available'.
inputs: None
outputs: Your Knowledge Base is created! Bedrock now has a pipeline that can: (1) read your documents from S3, (2) split them into chunks, (3) convert chunks to vectors, and (4) search those vectors when users ask questions.
how_to_verify: The status on the Knowledge Base detail page shows 'Ready' or 'Available' in green.
troubleshooting: If status shows 'Failed': check that your IAM user has AdministratorAccess. Try deleting it and creating again.

action: Copy the Knowledge Base ID
detailed_instructions: On the Knowledge Base detail page (you should be here after step 27), find the Knowledge Base ID near the top of the page. It's a string of letters and numbers like 'ABCDEFGHIJ' or 'QZ4KXYZ123'. Copy this value and save it - you'll need it soon.
inputs: Input: None - just read the page.
outputs: Output: Your Knowledge Base ID (a string like 'ABCDEFGHIJ'). This is a unique identifier for your RAG pipeline.
how_to_verify: You have the ID copied and saved somewhere (text file, notepad, etc).
troubleshooting: The ID is usually displayed prominently near the KB name at the top of the detail page.

action: Copy the Data Source ID
detailed_instructions: On the same Knowledge Base detail page, scroll down to the 'Data sources' section. You'll see the data source you created (named 'rag-documents-s3'). Find its Data Source ID - another string of letters and numbers. Copy this value and save it.
inputs: Input: None - just read the page.
outputs: Output: Your Data Source ID (a string like 'XXXXXXXXXX'). This identifies the connection between your S3 bucket and the Knowledge Base.
how_to_verify: You have the Data Source ID copied and saved.
troubleshooting: If you don't see a Data sources section, make sure you're on the KB detail page (click the KB name).

action: Sync the Knowledge Base to index your documents
detailed_instructions: On the Knowledge Base detail page, in the Data sources section, click the checkbox next to your data source to select it. Then click the 'Sync' button. This tells Bedrock to read all the documents in your S3 bucket, split them into chunks, convert each chunk into a vector, and store those vectors for searching. Wait for a green success banner to appear.
inputs: Input: None - the sync reads from the S3 bucket you configured in step 24.
outputs: Output: All your documents are now indexed and searchable. The vectors are stored in S3 Vectors.
how_to_verify: A green banner appears saying sync completed. You can also click 'Sync history' to see details - it should show the number of documents scanned and indexed.
troubleshooting: Sync time depends on how many documents you uploaded. 10 documents might take 30 seconds, 500 documents might take several minutes. If sync fails: check that your documents bucket has files in the category folders.

action: Redeploy the infrastructure with your Knowledge Base IDs
detailed_instructions: Go back to your terminal. Navigate to the cdk/ directory: cd path/to/AWSClaude/cdk. Run this command, replacing YOUR_KB_ID and YOUR_DS_ID with the actual values you copied in steps 28 and 29: cdk deploy RagToolStack -c knowledgeBaseId=YOUR_KB_ID -c dataSourceId=YOUR_DS_ID --require-approval broadening. Example: cdk deploy RagToolStack -c knowledgeBaseId=ABCDEFGHIJ -c dataSourceId=XXXXXXXXXX --require-approval broadening. Type 'y' if prompted.
inputs: Input: Knowledge Base ID from step 28 and Data Source ID from step 29.
outputs: Output: The Lambda functions are updated with your Knowledge Base IDs. They can now connect to the Knowledge Base to search documents. Without this step, the search and upload features would not work.
how_to_verify: Terminal shows 'RagToolStack' with a green checkmark. The deployment is much faster this time (1-2 min) because it's only updating environment variables, not creating new resources.
troubleshooting: If you get an error: double-check you're in the cdk/ directory. Make sure the KB_ID and DS_ID have no spaces or extra characters.

action: Install frontend dependencies
detailed_instructions: Navigate to the frontend folder: cd ../frontend (or cd path/to/AWSClaude/frontend). Run: npm install. This downloads React (the UI framework), TypeScript (the programming language), Tailwind CSS (the styling library), and all other frontend dependencies.
inputs: Input: None. The package.json file lists everything needed.
outputs: Output: A node_modules/ folder is created in the frontend/ directory containing all the downloaded libraries.
how_to_verify: Terminal shows 'added X packages' with no errors.
troubleshooting: If you see errors: delete the node_modules folder and package-lock.json file, then run npm install again.

action: Build the production website
detailed_instructions: From the frontend/ directory, run: npm run build. This compiles all the React/TypeScript source code into optimized HTML, JavaScript, and CSS files that a web browser can understand. The result goes into a 'dist/' folder.
inputs: Input: None. The build reads all files in frontend/src/.
outputs: Output: A dist/ folder is created containing: index.html (the main page), JavaScript bundles (the app logic), and CSS files (the styling). These are the files that will be uploaded to S3 and served to users.
how_to_verify: You see 'dist/' folder with files inside it. The terminal shows build completed without errors.
troubleshooting: If build fails with TypeScript errors: make sure you ran npm install first. If it fails with 'Cannot find module': delete node_modules and run npm install again.

action: Upload the website to S3
detailed_instructions: Run: aws s3 sync dist/ s3://YOUR_FRONTEND_BUCKET/ --delete. Replace YOUR_FRONTEND_BUCKET with the FrontendBucketName from step 18 (e.g. 123456789012-rag-frontend). The 'sync' command uploads all new/changed files. The '--delete' flag removes any old files that are no longer in the build.
inputs: Input: FrontendBucketName from step 18 (e.g. 123456789012-rag-frontend). Source: the dist/ folder from step 33.
outputs: Output: Your website files are now in the S3 bucket. CloudFront (the CDN) will serve these files to users who visit the DistributionUrl.
how_to_verify: Terminal shows 'upload: dist/index.html to s3://...' and similar lines for each file.
troubleshooting: If you get 'NoSuchBucket': double-check the bucket name from step 18.

action: Clear the CloudFront cache
detailed_instructions: Run: aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id YOUR_DIST_ID --paths '/*'. Replace YOUR_DIST_ID with the DistributionId from step 18 (e.g. E1234567890ABC). CloudFront caches (saves copies of) your website files globally. This command tells it to throw away old copies and fetch the fresh ones from S3.
inputs: Input: DistributionId from step 18 (e.g. E1234567890ABC).
outputs: Output: CloudFront will refresh its cached files globally. This takes 1-2 minutes to complete.
how_to_verify: Terminal shows a JSON response with 'Status': 'InProgress' and an 'Id'. The cache clear is happening in the background.
troubleshooting: If you get an error: check the DistributionId is correct. It starts with 'E' followed by letters and numbers.

action: Access the application in your browser
detailed_instructions: Open a web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Edge, etc). Go to the DistributionUrl from step 18. It looks like: https://d1234567890abc.cloudfront.net. You MUST be on the IP address you configured in step 13.
inputs: Input: DistributionUrl from step 18.
outputs: Output: The RAG Document Tool web application loads. You should see a blue header bar with the app name and 3 navigation tabs: 'Search & Generate', 'File Manager', and 'Audit'.
how_to_verify: The page loads and you see the application UI with three tabs.
troubleshooting: If you see a '403 Forbidden' error: your current IP address doesn't match what you put in config.ts in step 13. Go to whatismyip.com to check your current IP, update config.ts, and redeploy (step 31). If you see a blank page: wait 2 minutes for the CloudFront cache to clear (step 35), then refresh.

action: Test the File Manager
detailed_instructions: Click the 'File Manager' tab in the navigation bar. You should see your uploaded documents listed in a table, organized by category. Try uploading a new file: click 'Select File', choose a document from your computer, select a category from the dropdown, and click 'Upload File'.
inputs: Input: A document file (.pdf, .docx, or .txt) from your computer to test uploading.
outputs: Output: The File Manager page shows your existing documents. After uploading, a green success message appears and the new file shows up in the list.
how_to_verify: Documents from step 20 appear in the table. A test upload succeeds with a green message.
troubleshooting: If no documents appear: check that you uploaded to the correct bucket in step 20. If upload fails: check the Lambda function logs in CloudWatch (AWS Console > CloudWatch > Log groups > search for 'UploadFunction').

action: Test the Search feature
detailed_instructions: Click the 'Search & Generate' tab. In the text box, type a query related to your documents (e.g. 'What is our technical approach to cloud migration?'). Select a category from the dropdown (or leave as 'All Categories'). Select a writing tone (e.g. 'Professional'). Click 'Search for Relevant Documents'. Wait 5-10 seconds.
inputs: Input: A text query related to your uploaded documents. A category selection. A tone selection.
outputs: Output: A list of search results appears, each showing: the document filename, a relevance score (percentage), and a passage from the document explaining why it matched your query.
how_to_verify: Results appear with filenames, scores, and text passages. Each result has a checkbox for selection.
troubleshooting: If no results appear: (1) Make sure KB sync completed successfully in step 30. (2) Wait 2-3 minutes after sync - vectors may need time to become queryable. (3) Try a different query or 'All Categories'. (4) Check CloudWatch logs for the SearchFunction Lambda.

action: Test the Generation feature
detailed_instructions: From the search results in step 38, check the boxes next to one or more results you want to use as references. Click the green 'Proceed with Selected' button. Wait 10-30 seconds while Claude generates a draft document.
inputs: Input: Selected search results from step 38 (the passages become reference material for the AI).
outputs: Output: A generated draft document appears on screen, written in the tone you selected, using the style and content of the reference documents you chose. You can click 'Copy to Clipboard' to copy the text.
how_to_verify: A draft document appears below the search results. It should match the tone you selected and reference content from the selected documents.
troubleshooting: If generation fails or takes too long: the Lambda timeout might be too short for very long passages. Check CloudWatch logs for the GenerateFunction Lambda.

action: Test the Audit feature
detailed_instructions: Click the 'Audit' tab. Upload two files: (1) In 'Document to Audit' - upload the document you want checked, (2) In 'Criteria / Reference File' - upload a document containing the requirements or criteria. Click 'Run Audit'. Wait 1-2 minutes (this uses a more powerful AI model for thorough analysis).
inputs: Input: Two document files - one to audit and one containing criteria/requirements.
outputs: Output: A structured audit report showing: an overall score (PASS/PARTIAL/FAIL), each criterion extracted from the reference file, a verdict for each criterion (MEETS/PARTIALLY MEETS/DOES NOT MEET), evidence quotes from the audited document, and improvement recommendations.
how_to_verify: A color-coded audit report appears with individual criterion assessments and an overall score.
troubleshooting: Audit takes 1-2 minutes because it uses Claude Sonnet (a more powerful but slower model). If it times out: your documents may be very large. Check CloudWatch logs for the AuditFunction Lambda.

action: How to add new documents after initial setup
detailed_instructions: Upload the document to S3: aws s3 cp new-file.pdf s3://YOUR_BUCKET/tech-approach/ (use your DocumentsBucketName and appropriate category). Then sync the Knowledge Base: go to Bedrock > Knowledge bases > rag-tool-kb > select data source > click Sync. Or use CLI: aws bedrock-agent start-ingestion-job --knowledge-base-id YOUR_KB_ID --data-source-id YOUR_DS_ID. Wait for sync to complete. The new document is now searchable. NOTE: Documents uploaded through the File Manager UI automatically trigger a sync.
inputs: Input: New document file, DocumentsBucketName, and (for CLI sync) Knowledge Base ID and Data Source ID.
outputs: Output: The new document is indexed and becomes searchable in the tool.
how_to_verify: After sync completes, search for content in the new document and it should appear in results.
troubleshooting: CLI uploads need manual sync. UI uploads trigger sync automatically.

action: How to add a new document category
detailed_instructions: 1. Open cdk/lib/config.ts in a text editor. Find the CATEGORIES array. Add a new entry following the pattern of existing ones (id, label, s3Prefix with trailing slash, description). Save. 2. Redeploy: cd cdk && cdk deploy RagToolStack -c knowledgeBaseId=YOUR_KB_ID -c dataSourceId=YOUR_DS_ID --require-approval broadening. 3. Create the S3 folder: aws s3api put-object --bucket YOUR_BUCKET --key new-category/ --content-length 0. 4. Upload documents to the new folder. 5. Sync the KB (step 41). 6. Rebuild and redeploy frontend: cd frontend && npm run build && aws s3 sync dist/ s3://YOUR_FRONTEND_BUCKET/ --delete
inputs: Input: New category name and description. All IDs from earlier steps.
outputs: Output: A new category appears in all dropdown menus throughout the application.
how_to_verify: The new category shows up in the Search and File Manager dropdowns after redeploying the frontend.
troubleshooting: Make sure the s3Prefix ends with a forward slash (e.g. 'cost-volume/').

action: How to change the allowed IP addresses
detailed_instructions: 1. Open cdk/lib/config.ts in a text editor. Find the ALLOWED_IPS array. Add or remove IP addresses (each must end with /32 for a single IP). Save. 2. Redeploy: cd cdk && cdk deploy RagToolStack -c knowledgeBaseId=YOUR_KB_ID -c dataSourceId=YOUR_DS_ID --require-approval broadening
inputs: Input: New IP addresses to allow (with /32 suffix).
outputs: Output: The WAF firewall is updated. New IPs can access the app, removed IPs are blocked.
how_to_verify: Try accessing the app from a newly-allowed IP. Try accessing from a removed IP (should get 403).
troubleshooting: Changes take effect within a few minutes after deployment.
